title: Claude Can Now Control Your Computer (This Changes Everything)

eval.summary: A short-form promotional video (likely a social media reel) covering Claude's computer use feature and a 'dispatch' capability for remote computer control via mobile. The content is surface-level: no code, no architecture, no integration patterns. It accurately describes Claude computer use (real Anthropic feature) but frames it as consumer magic rather than engineering. The 'dispatch' framing may refer to Claude's desktop app or a third-party wrapper — no technical specificity is given. Zero actionable integration detail. The video exists to drive comment engagement ('comment WTF') for a lead magnet funnel. For the Veritas stack, Claude computer use is already a known Anthropic capability on the radar; the question of integrating it into Alan OS or AgentOS workflows is non-trivial and requires explicit P4 human gate design, P6 auth hardening, and P3 token efficiency measurement. This video contributes nothing to that analysis.
eval.fit_rationale: Claude computer use is Anthropic-native and theoretically relevant to Alan OS automation, but this video provides zero engineering depth to inform an integration decision.

stack_evaluation.replaces_or_complements: would theoretically complement n8n + Outlook COM automation in Alan OS, but no actionable signal here

Fails P7: computer use overlaps heavily with existing automation layer and would require a full BUILD_OR_EXTEND.md before any implementation — this video cannot seed that doc. Content is engagement-bait with no technical substance; re-evaluating in 30 days adds no value since the underlying Anthropic capability is already known.

title: stop building Agents and start building something that lasts.

eval.summary: Jake Van Clief argues that AI agents are overbuilt and that the best 'agent' is a markdown file plus scripts routed through that markdown file, organized via folder structures. He cites Anthropic's Claude Code as evidence that the industry's best practitioners are moving away from complex agent frameworks toward simpler, file-based orchestration. The argument is directionally aligned with how Alan OS is already built (CLAUDE.md, .lux/ folder structure, FastAPI scripts), but the video offers no new tooling, no measurable technique, no API surface, no token-efficiency data, and no integration path. It is essentially a contrarian opinion piece dressed as technical insight. The 'best agent is a markdown file' thesis is already operationalized in the Veritas stack — CLAUDE.md IS the agent OS. Van Clief provides zero acceptance-test-passing evidence for P3 (token efficiency lift), no specific tooling for P7 evaluation, and no exportable artifact. The content validates existing architectural decisions without adding anything actionable.
eval.fit_rationale: The thesis is already implemented in Alan OS via CLAUDE.md and .lux/ structure; no new capability, tooling, or integration path is offered.

stack_evaluation.replaces_or_complements: nothing — validates existing architecture without adding capability
stack_evaluation.reasoning: Fails P3 — no token efficiency data or eval rubric presented, making ADOPT/EVALUATE impossible by acceptance test. Fails P7 — the 'markdown-as-orchestration' pattern has ≥90% overlap with CLAUDE.md and alan_os_server.py already in the stack, so no new file or component is justified. The content is motivational framing of a pattern Veritas already ships.

No token efficiency data, no export API, no webhook surface mentioned.

title: How to build a Second Brain (prompt) #claude #claudecode #openclaw

eval.summary: A 30-minute YouTube pitch selling a $15 'second brain' prompt package that instructs Claude Code to set up an Obsidian vault with auto-logging and semantic file linking. The core concept — session-end memory dumps to a local folder, loaded at session start via agent config files — is a pattern Veritas already implements via CLAUDE.md, session-log, and .lux/data/*.json (P8). The 'semantic linking' claim is vague and unverified. The $15 purchase is a black-box prompt; no API, no export path, no documented schema (P1 fail). There is no token-efficiency measurement (P3 fail). The 'Andre Karpathy uses this system' claim is unverifiable marketing. The Obsidian dependency adds a GUI-only local store with no documented write API in the described workflow. Nothing here extends VIE, improves orchestration, or adds a capability the stack lacks. The concept is valid but already solved in the Veritas stack. Buying a prompt to do what CLAUDE.md + session-log already does violates P7 (overlap ≥ 60% with existing memory surfaces).
eval.fit_rationale: The described pattern is already implemented via CLAUDE.md, session-log, and .lux/data/*.json; no new capability is introduced.

P3 fail: zero token-efficiency measurement is provided; the '30% reduction' threshold cannot be evaluated. The $15 cost is irrelevant — integration time and schema lock-in are the real costs.
